title: Distinct Subsequences
problem_statement: Given two strings s and t, return the number of distinct subsequences of s that equal t. A subsequence of a string is a new string formed from the original string by deleting some characters without changing the order of the remaining characters.

examples.explanation: There are three ways to delete one 'b' from s to make t.
There are five distinct subsequences of s that form "bag".

constraints: s and t consist of lowercase English letters.
intuition: For each prefix of s and t, count how many ways t can be formed by either matching the current character or skipping the current character in s.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Recurse on both strings. If the current characters match, add ways from matching and skipping; otherwise skip the current character in s.
approaches.why_not: Exponential due to exploring all subsequence decisions. Many subproblems overlap.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Memoize the number of ways to form t[j:] from s[i:] to avoid recomputing the same prefix states.
approaches.why_not: Bottom-up is iterative and avoids recursion overhead, but this is still efficient.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Use a 1D dp array where dp[j] counts how many ways t[0..j-1] can be formed from the processed prefix of s.
approaches.why_not: This is the standard way to optimize space while keeping the same correctness.

interview_qa.q: What is the dp state in the optimized solution?
interview_qa.a: dp[j] stores the number of ways to form the first j characters of t from the processed prefix of s.

interview_qa.q: Why iterate backward when using 1D dp?
interview_qa.a: To avoid overwriting dp[j-1] before it is used for the current s character.
We can either use this match and add dp[j-1], or skip s[i] and keep dp[j].
There is exactly one way to form an empty t from any prefix of s: delete all remaining characters.
